summarization.a: Multi-axis chart showing the Gibbs free energy of removal (ΔG_rm at 80 °C) as a function of nitrogen coverage for the Ni/N system. Dual y-axes compare formamidine and formic acid chemistries across multiple crystallographic facets, with bulk reference energies indicated for context.
summarization.b: Multi-axis chart showing removal energies versus nitrogen coverage for the Cu/N system. The data compare facet-dependent trends and reveal generally less favorable removal energetics than the corresponding Ni/N system.
summarization.c: Multi-axis chart showing removal energies versus oxygen coverage for the Ni/O system. Several surface reconstructions are explicitly labeled, including (100) p(2×2) and (110) missing-row structures, enabling comparison of reconstruction-dependent stability.
summarization.d: Multi-axis chart showing removal energies versus oxygen coverage for the Cu/O system. The figure highlights reconstruction-dependent trends, including (110) (2×1)-PR, and compares surface removal energetics against bulk reference states.

a.question: What does the slope of each ΔG_rm vs. θ_N curve represent?
a.answer: It reflects how the removal energy changes with nitrogen coverage, indicating the sensitivity of surface stability to adsorbate density.

a.question: In the Ni/N system, does the Gibbs free energy of removal generally become more favorable (more negative) as nitrogen coverage increases?
a.answer: No, the energy generally increases (becomes more positive and less favorable) as coverage increases.

c.question: Which surface reconstructions are labeled for the Ni/O system, and which reconstruction appears most energetically favorable at low oxygen coverage?
c.answer: Subfigure (c) labels p(2×2) and c(2×2) reconstructions on the (100) surface and (2×1)-MR and (3×1)-MR missing-row reconstructions on the (110) surface. At low oxygen coverage, the (110) (2×1)-MR reconstruction shows the lowest ΔG_rm values, indicating the most favorable removal energetics among the labeled structures.

d.question: What does the similarity between formamidine and formic acid ΔG_rm trends imply?
d.answer: It suggests that both chemistries follow comparable surface reaction energetics. Either pathway could drive controlled removal if conditions are tuned for the appropriate surface state, giving flexibility in precursor choice for ALE design.
